race_name: Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH)

top_categories.workout_context: 7 miles on paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
When someone attacks on paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH)'s demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
Base fitness for Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH). Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.
At Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH), surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH)'s paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 7 miles.
Varied gradients on paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained demand climbing versatility. Sit the shallow stuff, stand the steep stuff, switch without thinking.
608ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH). Train it until it feels boring.
Low-cadence force work for 4,618ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH), that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.

race_overlay.heat: August in Gorham, New Hampshire — Mid-August at the Auto Road base starts mild (60-75F) but conditions deteriorate rapidly with altitude. Mt. Washington holds the record for the highest wind gust directly measured at the Earth's surface: 231 mph. The summit's yearly average temperature is 33.9F — no temperature above 72F has ever been recorded there. Even on a calm August race day, summit conditions can include fog, rain, high winds, and temperatures 20-30 degrees colder than the base. The weather observatory on the summit exists specifically because this mountain produces some of the most extreme weather on Earth. Begin heat acclimatization 10–14 days before Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH) — 20–30min sauna sessions or midday rides in full kit. Pre-load sodium 48 hours out. Target 500–750ml/hr with electrolytes on race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Standard hydration and fueling. One bottle per hour minimum. Gel or bar every 45 minutes at race intensity.
race_overlay.altitude: Moderate altitude at Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH). Arrive 2–3 days early. Reduce intensity expectations by 5–10%. Hydrate aggressively.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 7 miles of paved mountain auto road — extreme sustained gradient in Gorham, New Hampshire.
